title: Filing Your First Claim
desc: Step-by-step from DD-214 to decision letter -- everything you need to file right the first time

sections.heading: :shield: Before You Start: Gather Your Records
sections.body:
Your **DD-214 (Member 4 copy)** is the single most important document in your claim. Without it, nothing moves. It proves your service dates, duty stations, and character of discharge.

> [!important] If you don't have your DD-214, request it through the **National Personnel Records Center (NPRC)** at eVetRecs immediately. This can take weeks -- start NOW.

---

**Beyond the DD-214, gather:**
- **Service treatment records (STRs)** -- every sick call, every complaint, every diagnosis
- **Private medical records** related to your conditions
- **Personnel records** showing deployments, MOS assignments, and hazardous duty

> [!tip] Under `38 CFR 3.303(a)`, VA must consider "the places, types and circumstances of service." Your deployment orders, MOS records, and hazardous duty assignments help establish the environment that caused or worsened your conditions -- even if your STRs are thin.

> [!warning] VA has been known to lose records. **Make copies of EVERYTHING.** Keep your own complete file -- digital and physical. If VA loses it and you don't have a backup, you're starting from scratch.

sections.heading: :tools: Step 1: Create Your VA.gov Account
sections.body:
Your VA.gov account is mission control for your entire claim. Set it up right and you'll save yourself headaches down the road.

**Do this now:**
1. Go to **VA.gov** and create a **Login.gov** or **ID.me** account
2. Verify your **contact information** and **mailing address**
3. Set up **direct deposit** details (this is where your money goes)
4. Check whether VA already has your **service treatment records** on file

> [!check] Before you file anything, log in and view your VA medical records and any previously submitted documents. Know what VA already has -- and what's missing.

> [!warning] If your STRs are missing from VA's records, request them separately through NPRC. But **do NOT let missing STRs delay your filing** -- file your Intent to File (next step) immediately and gather records in parallel.

sections.heading: :flag: Step 2: File an Intent to File (VA Form 21-0966)
sections.body:
This is the **single most important step** most veterans skip -- and it costs them thousands.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 3.155(b)`, filing an **Intent to File (ITF)** locks in your effective date -- the date from which VA will pay benefits if your claim is granted. You then have **one full year** to submit the complete application.

---

**Three ways to file an ITF:**

| Method | How | Notes |
|--------|-----|-------|
| **Online** | Start an application on VA.gov | System saves ITF automatically |
| **Paper** | Submit `VA Form 21-0966` | Mail or hand-deliver |
| **Phone** | Call **1-800-827-1000** | Rep must document it per `38 CFR 3.155(b)(1)(iii)` |

The ITF only needs to identify the **general benefit** you're seeking (compensation). It does **not** need to list specific conditions.

> [!check] File your Intent to File TODAY -- even if you're months away from submitting your full claim. Under `38 CFR 3.400`, your effective date is the later of the claim date or the date entitlement arose. **Every day you wait is money left on the table.**

> [!quote] A veteran who files an ITF on January 1st and submits their full claim on June 1st gets back pay to January 1st. A veteran who skips the ITF and files on June 1st loses five months of compensation -- potentially thousands of dollars.

sections.heading: :link: Step 3: Consider Getting a VSO or Representative
sections.body:
You don't have to do this alone -- and the help is **free**.

> [!tip] Veterans Service Organizations (VSOs) like the **DAV**, **VFW**, **American Legion**, and state-level organizations have trained claims agents who know the system inside out. They can access VA's internal systems to track your claim in real time.

---

**What a good VSO will do for you:**
- Review your service records and **identify conditions you may have missed**
- Ensure your paperwork is **complete and properly formatted**
- **Advocate on your behalf** throughout the process
- Track your claim through VA's internal systems

**How to appoint representation:**

| Form | Use For |
|------|---------|
| `VA Form 21-22` | Appoint a VSO |
| `VA Form 21-22a` | Appoint a private attorney or claims agent |

> [!check] You can change representatives at any time. Don't feel locked in -- if your VSO isn't responsive, switch. Your claim is too important for someone who won't return calls.

> [!warning] Not all VSOs are created equal. Ask other veterans in your area who they recommend. A bad VSO is worse than no VSO -- they can miss deadlines and give wrong advice.

sections.heading: :search: Step 4: Identify All Your Conditions
sections.body:
Here's the rule: **claim everything, let VA decide.** Do not self-eliminate. Your job is to put it on paper; VA's job is to adjudicate it.

> [!important] List every condition you believe is connected to your military service -- both obvious injuries and conditions that developed over time. If it started in service or got worse because of service, claim it.

---

**Commonly overlooked conditions:**
- **Tinnitus** (ringing in the ears) -- one of the most commonly rated disabilities
- **Sleep apnea** -- especially common after deployments
- **Migraines** -- often linked to TBI or stress
- **Mental health** (PTSD, anxiety, depression) -- no shame, huge impact on ratings
- **Flat feet** (pes planus) -- worsened by years of rucking
- **GERD/acid reflux** -- often secondary to medications or stress
- **Joint conditions** that worsened over years of service

> [!check] Think about **secondary conditions** -- disabilities caused or aggravated by an already service-connected condition. Under `38 CFR 3.310(a)`, disability "proximately due to or the result of a service-connected disease or injury shall be service connected." Under `3.310(b)`, even **aggravation** of a nonservice-connected condition by a service-connected one qualifies.

> [!quote] A veteran with service-connected PTSD develops sleep apnea and GERD from medication side effects. Both are potentially secondary claims under 38 CFR 3.310 -- adding significant combined rating on top of the PTSD.

sections.heading: :target: Step 5: Build Your Evidence Package
sections.body:
This is where claims are won or lost. For each condition, you need **three things** to establish service connection under `38 CFR 3.303`:

| Element | What It Proves | Key Evidence |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Current diagnosis** | You have the condition now | Medical records, doctor's letter |
| **In-service event** | Something happened during service | STRs, buddy statements, deployment records |
| **Nexus** | The two are connected | Medical opinion letter, IMO |

---

**Building each element:**

**In-service evidence:** Service treatment records showing complaints, treatment, or diagnosis are powerful. If your STRs are silent, **buddy statements** from fellow service members who witnessed your condition or the event fill that gap.

**The nexus opinion:** Your treating physician can write an opinion letter, or you can get an **Independent Medical Opinion (IMO)** from a specialist.

> [!important] The magic words are **"at least as likely as not"** -- meaning a 50% or greater probability. This is the legal standard. Make sure your doctor uses this exact phrase.

> [!tip] Under `38 CFR 3.303(d)`, service connection can be granted for **any disease diagnosed after discharge** when all the evidence establishes it was incurred in service. You don't need a diagnosis from while you were in -- you need a nexus connecting it back.

> [!warning] Don't rely on VA to build your case for you. The stronger your initial submission, the faster and more favorable the outcome.

sections.heading: :doc: Step 6: Submit VA Form 21-526EZ
sections.body:
This is the main event. File online at **VA.gov** using `VA Form 21-526EZ` (Application for Disability Compensation).

> [!check] List ALL conditions and upload EVERY piece of supporting evidence. Do not hold anything back for later.

---

**For each condition, include:**
- The **specific diagnosis** (not just symptoms -- "lumbar degenerative disc disease," not "back pain")
- **When you first noticed it** during service
- **What in-service event or exposure** caused it
- **How it affects your daily life and work** right now

> [!tip] Be specific and vivid in your descriptions. "I cannot sit at my desk for more than 30 minutes without severe lower back pain" is infinitely better than "my back hurts."

After submission, VA sends an acknowledgment letter and begins the **development phase**. Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)`, VA has a **duty to assist** you in obtaining evidence -- including service records, VA medical records, and scheduling C&P exams.

> [!warning] VA's duty to assist is real but limited. They'll request records and schedule exams, but they won't build your argument for you. The veterans who submit strong, complete packages up front get faster and better results.

sections.heading: :alert: Step 7: The C&P Exam
sections.body:
For most conditions, VA will schedule a **Compensation & Pension exam**. This is NOT a treatment appointment -- the examiner's job is to document severity and provide a nexus opinion.

> [!danger] Military culture teaches us to minimize pain and push through. The C&P exam is where that instinct costs you money. Describe your WORST days, not your best.

---

**Bring to every C&P exam:**
- List of **all medications** with dosages
- Your **supporting documents**
- Notes about **how your condition affects daily activities**
- Specific examples of **functional limitations**

> [!tip] If the examiner's report contains errors or doesn't reflect what you told them, you can submit a **rebuttal statement** or request a new exam. Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)(4)`, VA has a duty to provide an **adequate examination** -- one that considers the full record and provides a reasoned opinion.

> [!check] Read our **C&P Exam Survival Guide** for the complete breakdown on how to prepare, what examiners test, and what to do if it goes wrong.

sections.heading: :star: Step 8: Track, Respond, and Don't Give Up
sections.body:
The average processing time is **3-6 months**, but complex claims can take longer. Stay engaged.

> [!important] Monitor your claim status on **VA.gov** regularly. If VA sends a development letter requesting additional evidence, **respond promptly**. You typically have 30 days before VA may decide on what's in the file -- though you have up to one year under `38 CFR 3.159(b)(1)`.

---

**If your claim is denied or rated lower than expected, you have three appeal options:**

| Lane | Form | Best When |
|------|------|-----------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | `VA Form 20-0995` | You have **new evidence** to submit |
| **Higher-Level Review** | `VA Form 20-0996` | You believe VA made an **error of fact or law** |
| **Board Appeal** | `VA Form 10182` | You want a **Veterans Law Judge** to review |

> [!check] You have **one year** from the decision date to file in any lane while preserving your effective date. Don't let the clock run out.

> [!tip] A denial is NOT the end. Many successful claims were initially denied. The appeal system exists because VA gets it wrong -- a lot. Stay in the fight.

sections.heading: :fire: Common First-Claim Mistakes
sections.body:
These are the mistakes that cost veterans the most money and time. Avoid every single one.

> [!danger] **Not filing an Intent to File first** -- this is the #1 money-losing mistake. Every day without an ITF is a day of back pay you'll never recover.

---

**The full list of claim-killers:**

| Mistake | Why It Hurts |
|---------|--------------|
| Skipping the ITF | Lose months or years of back pay |
| Filing too few conditions | You left ratings (and money) on the table |
| Submitting without evidence | VA won't build your case for you |
| Missing the C&P exam | Almost always an automatic denial |
| Describing best-day symptoms | Examiner documents lower severity |
| No nexus letter with weak STRs | No link between service and condition |
| Ignoring secondary conditions | Often worth more than the primary under `38 CFR 3.310` |
| Accepting denial as final | You have appeal rights -- use them |

> [!check] The veterans who win are the ones who **prepare thoroughly**, **file strategically**, and **never stop fighting** for what they've earned.

> [!quote] "I almost didn't file for tinnitus because I thought ringing ears was normal after the military. That 10% rating pushed me from 60% to 70% combined -- unlocking dependent pay and an extra $400/month." -- V2V Community Member

title: C&P Exam Survival Guide
desc: What to expect, bring, and say at your Compensation & Pension exam -- and what to do if it goes wrong

sections.heading: :book: What Is a C&P Exam?
sections.body:
A Compensation & Pension exam is VA's way of evaluating your claimed conditions. This is **not a treatment appointment** -- the examiner's sole job is to document your current severity and render a medical opinion on service connection.

> [!important] The exam report goes directly to the VA rater who decides your claim. What the examiner writes is often the single most influential piece of evidence in your entire file.

---

Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)(4)`, VA must provide a medical examination when the record contains:
- Evidence of a **current disability**
- An **in-service event**
- An indication the two **may be associated**

The examiner fills out a **Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ)** -- a standardized form specific to your condition that captures exactly the data VA raters need to assign a rating.

> [!tip] The DBQ is a checklist. The examiner checks boxes and fills in numbers. Understanding what's on YOUR specific DBQ tells you exactly what the examiner will test -- and what you need to be prepared to describe.

> [!warning] Some examiners spend as little as 10-15 minutes with you. That's not much time to capture the full picture of a disability you live with 24/7. Come prepared to communicate efficiently.

sections.heading: :shield: What to Bring
sections.body:
Walk into that exam room locked and loaded with documentation. The examiner may or may not have read your file -- be ready either way.

**Your C&P exam kit:**
- **Government-issued photo ID**
- **Medication list** -- every med, dosage, and frequency
- **Private medical records** not yet submitted to VA
- **Written symptom summary** -- your condition in your own words
- **Buddy statement copies** -- corroboration from people who see your daily struggles
- **Worst-day notes** -- specific examples of your most severe symptoms
- **Condition timeline** -- onset in service through present day

> [!check] The written summary is your secret weapon. Exams are stressful and you WILL forget key details under pressure. Having your symptoms and limitations written down ensures nothing gets missed.

---

**Make your summary specific and functional:**

| Vague (Don't Say) | Specific (Say This) |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| "My back hurts" | "I cannot lift my children" |
| "I have trouble sleeping" | "I wake up 3-4 times per night from pain" |
| "Work is hard" | "I miss 3-4 days of work per month due to flare-ups" |
| "I can't do much" | "I cannot stand for more than 10 minutes" |

> [!tip] Functional impact is what drives ratings. The examiner needs to know what you **cannot do** -- not just what hurts.

sections.heading: :target: How to Describe Your Symptoms
sections.body:
Describe your condition on your **worst days, not your best**. This is the single most important mindset shift for your C&P exam.

> [!danger] Many veterans unconsciously minimize their symptoms because of military culture -- we were trained to push through pain and say "I'm fine." The C&P exam is NOT the place for that. Minimizing here costs you money, period.

---

**The before and after of symptom description:**

| Instead Of | Say |
|-----------|-----|
| "My back hurts sometimes" | "My back pain prevents me from sitting for more than 20 minutes. I have flare-ups 3-4 times a week where the pain is so severe I can't bend to tie my shoes. I wake up 2-3 times a night from the pain. On my worst days, I can barely get out of bed." |

**Be specific about these five dimensions:**
- **Frequency** -- how often symptoms occur
- **Duration** -- how long episodes last
- **Severity** -- pain scale, functional limitations
- **Impact** -- what you can no longer do
- **Flare-ups** -- how often, how severe, how long

> [!check] Practice describing your symptoms out loud before the exam. Use the five dimensions above as your framework. If you can hit all five for each condition, you've given the examiner everything they need.

> [!tip] Bring a spouse, caregiver, or buddy to the exam if possible. They can provide a third-party perspective on how your condition affects daily life -- and they keep you honest about not minimizing.

sections.heading: :search: What Examiners Test for Common Conditions
sections.body:
Each condition has a specific DBQ that drives what the examiner evaluates. Knowing what they're looking for lets you prepare the right information.

---

**PTSD/Mental Health** (`DC 9411`):
- Specific **stressor events** and their details
- Symptom **frequency and severity**
- **Occupational and social impairment** level
- Sleep disturbance, hypervigilance, avoidance behaviors
- Impact on **work and relationships**

> [!important] Be open about suicidal ideation if it applies. The examiner is not there to judge -- they are there to document. Holding back on mental health symptoms is the #1 reason PTSD claims get underrated.

---

**Back/Spine** (`DC 5237/5242`):
- **Range of motion** testing: forward flexion, extension, lateral flexion, rotation
- Pain on **active and passive motion** per `38 CFR 4.59`
- Additional loss during **flare-ups**

**Knees** (`DC 5260/5261`):
- **Flexion and extension** range of motion
- **Stability testing**
- Pain on **weight-bearing and nonweight-bearing**
- Opposite knee tested for **comparison** (required under `38 CFR 4.59`)

**Sleep Apnea** (`DC 6847`):
- Whether you use a **CPAP**
- Frequency of **breathing interruptions**
- **Daytime hypersomnolence**

> [!check] Bring your **sleep study results** to the exam. The examiner needs objective data to confirm severity.

> [!tip] Look up the blank DBQ for your specific condition on VA's website before the exam. It shows you exactly what checkboxes the examiner will fill out -- so you know what to talk about.

sections.heading: :doc: Understanding DBQs (Disability Benefits Questionnaires)
sections.body:
DBQs are the standardized forms examiners fill out during your C&P exam. Each one maps directly to **diagnostic codes and rating criteria** in `38 CFR Part 4`. Understanding your DBQ is like having the answer key before the test.

> [!important] You can find blank DBQ forms on VA's website. Review yours before the exam so you know exactly what will be measured and can prepare your answers accordingly.

---

**For musculoskeletal conditions, the DBQ captures:**
- Range of motion **in degrees**
- **Pain points** during testing
- Functional loss factors under `38 CFR 4.40` and `4.45`: weakness, excess fatigability, incoordination, pain on movement
- **Flare-up impact** -- estimated additional loss
- Additional loss after **repetitive use testing**

**For mental health conditions, the DBQ uses:**
- **Occupational and social impairment levels** that map directly to the General Rating Formula for Mental Disorders
- Checkboxes for **specific symptoms** (intrusive thoughts, nightmares, irritability, etc.)
- An **overall impairment level** assignment

> [!tip] The mental health DBQ impairment levels are the rating tiers: 0%, 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 100%. Each tier has specific language about occupational/social impairment. Know which tier matches your actual functioning.

> [!check] Print the blank DBQ for each of your claimed conditions. Go through it line by line and write down what your answer would be. Bring those notes to the exam.

sections.heading: :alert: Common Mistakes to Avoid
sections.body:
These mistakes tank claims every single day. Don't be that veteran.

> [!danger] **Don't say "I'm fine" out of habit.** Don't let pride prevent you from being honest about your limitations. The examiner writes down what you say -- if you say you're fine, that's what goes in the report.

---

**The C&P exam mistake hall of shame:**

| Mistake | What Happens |
|---------|--------------|
| **Skipping the exam** | Almost guarantees a denial |
| **Arguing with the examiner** | They're documenting, not deciding -- stay calm and factual |
| **Exaggerating symptoms** | Examiners detect inconsistencies; undermines ALL your credibility |
| **Taking pain meds before** | Masks your symptoms so the examiner sees a better picture than reality |
| **Assuming they read your file** | Some have, some haven't -- explain your full history regardless |
| **Minimizing out of pride** | Lower documented severity = lower rating = less money |

> [!warning] If you can't make the appointment, call to **reschedule immediately**. A no-show is treated as a failure to report -- and VA can deny your claim based solely on that.

> [!check] Be **truthful but thorough** about your worst days. There's a critical difference between exaggerating and fully describing your actual limitations. The examiner needs the complete picture, not the brave face.

sections.heading: :gavel: Flare-ups and Functional Loss: The DeLuca Factors
sections.body:
This is one of the most powerful tools in your arsenal -- and most veterans have never heard of it.

> [!important] The CAVC case **DeLuca v. Brown** established that VA must consider **functional loss beyond just what shows up on a range-of-motion test**. If you're having a good day at the exam, the DeLuca factors protect you.

---

**The legal framework:**
- `38 CFR 4.40`: Disability includes functional loss from **pain, weakness, and fatigability**
- `38 CFR 4.45`: Examiner must assess **excess fatigability, incoordination, and pain on movement**
- `38 CFR 4.59`: Painful motion with joint pathology is **"productive of disability"** -- actually painful, unstable, or malaligned joints are entitled to **at least the minimum compensable rating**

> [!check] If your condition flares up and you have worse symptoms than what the examiner sees in the office, **say so explicitly**. The examiner is required to ask about flare-ups and estimate additional functional loss during them. If they don't ask, volunteer the information.

**Example of what to say:**

> [!quote] "Today my knee bends to 90 degrees, but during a flare-up -- which happens 3-4 times a month and lasts 2-3 days -- I can barely bend it past 45 degrees and I can't use stairs."

> [!tip] The examiner must document flare-up frequency, duration, and estimated additional range-of-motion loss. If the final report doesn't include this information, the exam is **inadequate** and you can challenge it.

sections.heading: :eye: After the Exam: Review and Respond
sections.body:
The exam is done, but your work isn't. The examiner submits their report to VA within a few days to two weeks. **Get a copy and review it line by line.**

> [!check] Request a copy of your C&P exam report through VA.gov or by calling VA. Do this as soon as possible -- don't wait for the rating decision.

---

**Review the report for these issues:**
- **Factual errors** -- wrong dates, incorrect medical history
- **Missing symptoms** you reported during the exam
- **Inadequate testing** -- no passive motion testing, no flare-up assessment
- **Negative nexus opinion** that contradicts the evidence without explanation
- **Failure to consider** your lay statements and buddy statements

> [!warning] Examiner reports frequently contain errors. Conditions you described in detail may be summarized as "veteran reports mild pain." Dates may be wrong. Testing you know happened may not be documented. Don't assume the report is accurate.

**Your options if the report has problems:**

| Issue | Response |
|-------|----------|
| Factual errors | Submit a **rebuttal statement** pointing out specific inaccuracies |
| Inadequate testing | **Request a new examination** |
| Wrong nexus opinion | Submit a **private DBQ or IMO** from your own provider |

> [!tip] A private DBQ from your own doctor carries the same weight as a VA C&P exam. If the VA examiner got it wrong, your doctor can fill out the same form with the correct findings.

sections.heading: :gavel: When to Request a New Exam
sections.body:
Under `38 CFR 3.159`, VA has a **duty to provide an adequate examination**. If your exam was deficient, you have the right to demand a new one.

> [!important] An inadequate exam is not just annoying -- it's a legal error that can be the basis for overturning a denial on appeal.

---

**An exam is inadequate when:**
- The examiner **didn't review** your claims file or STRs
- Range of motion wasn't tested in **all required planes**
- Pain on **passive motion** wasn't assessed (required by `38 CFR 4.59`)
- The examiner didn't ask about or account for **flare-ups** (required under DeLuca)
- The negative nexus opinion **lacks rationale** beyond a bare conclusion
- The examiner relied on an **inaccurate factual premise**

> [!check] If you received an inadequate exam and your claim is denied, **cite the specific inadequacy in your appeal**. BVA regularly remands cases for new examinations when the original was deficient.

> [!tip] A **Higher-Level Review** (`VA Form 20-0996`) can identify duty-to-assist errors -- including inadequate examinations -- and return the case for correction without you needing to go all the way to the Board.

> [!quote] BVA remands thousands of cases every year specifically because C&P exams were inadequate. Common reasons: no flare-up assessment, no passive range-of-motion testing, and nexus opinions that say "not related" without explaining why.

title: Understanding VA Math
desc: How combined ratings actually work -- with real examples, the bilateral factor, and key benefit thresholds

sections.heading: :brain: Why 50% + 30% Doesn't Equal 80%
sections.body:
If you thought VA ratings were simple addition, welcome to the most frustrating math in the federal government. **50% + 30% = 70%** in VA math -- not 80%.

> [!important] VA uses a **"whole person" theory** codified in `38 CFR 4.25`. Instead of adding percentages, each rating is applied to the **remaining "healthy" portion** of your body.

---

Think of it this way:
- You start at **100% healthy**
- A 50% rating means you're now at **50% efficiency**
- The next rating applies to that **remaining 50%**, not to the full 100%

The Combined Ratings Table in `38 CFR 4.25` spells out the exact math. The regulation states: *"the efficiency of the individual as affected first by the most disabling condition, then by the less disabling condition, then by other less disabling conditions, if any, in the order of severity."*

> [!warning] This is why the order matters -- you **always start with the highest rating**. The sequence changes the intermediate math even though the final combined value is the same per the table.

> [!tip] Understanding VA math helps you make strategic decisions about which claims to file next. Sometimes a 10% increase on an existing rating is worth more than a brand new 10% claim.

sections.heading: :chart: The Formula: Step by Step
sections.body:
Here's the actual calculation VA uses. Start with your highest rating and apply each subsequent rating to the remaining healthy percentage.

> [!check] Follow along with this example: **Two disabilities at 50% and 30%**

---

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Start at 100% efficiency | 100% healthy |
| 2 | Apply 50% rating | 50% disabled, **50% remaining** |
| 3 | Apply 30% to remaining 50% | 50 x 0.30 = **15% additional** |
| 4 | Total disability | 50 + 15 = **65%** |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** |

So **50% + 30% = 70% combined** -- not 80%.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 4.25(a)`, combined values ending in **5 are always adjusted upward**. A value of 65% rounds to 70%, but 64% rounds down to 60%. That single percentage point can mean hundreds of dollars per month.

> [!tip] You can verify any combined rating using the Combined Ratings Table in `38 CFR 4.25`. Find your first rating on the left column, your second rating across the top, and the intersection gives you the combined value before rounding.

sections.heading: :chart: Worked Example: Three Ratings
sections.body:
Let's run a more complex scenario. **Three service-connected disabilities rated at 60%, 40%, and 20%.**

| Step | Action | Disability | Remaining |
|------|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Start | 0% | 100% |
| 2 | Apply 60% | 60% | 40% |
| 3 | Apply 40% to 40% remaining | 60 + 16 = 76% | 24% |
| 4 | Apply 20% to 24% remaining | 76 + 4.8 = **80.8%** | 19.2% |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **80% combined** | -- |

The combined ratings table in `38 CFR 4.25` confirms: 60 combined with 40 = 76, then 76 combined with 20 = 81, which rounds to **80%**.

> [!warning] Notice the **diminishing returns**. That 20% disability only added about 5 points to the combined rating. This is why pursuing **higher individual ratings** often matters more than adding small new claims.

> [!tip] If you're at 76% combined before that 20% rating, you're already rounding to 80%. Adding the 20% doesn't actually change your final combined rating in this case. **Always calculate before you file** to understand the real impact.

> [!check] Use an online VA combined rating calculator to model different scenarios before deciding what to file next. Know your numbers.

sections.heading: :chart: Worked Example: Four Ratings
sections.body:
Here's where diminishing returns really bite. **Four disabilities rated at 50%, 30%, 20%, and 10%.**

| Step | Action | Running Total | Remaining |
|------|--------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | Apply 50% | 50% | 50% |
| 2 | Apply 30% to 50% | 50 + 15 = 65% | 35% |
| 3 | Apply 20% to 35% | 65 + 7 = 72% | 28% |
| 4 | Apply 10% to 28% | 72 + 2.8 = **74.8%** | 25.2% |
| 5 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** | -- |

> [!warning] Four separate ratings totaling 110% in "normal math" only produce **70% combined** in VA math. The diminishing returns are brutal at the higher end.

> [!important] Per `38 CFR 4.25(b)`, "the conversion to the nearest degree divisible by 10 will be done only once per rating decision, will follow the combining of all disabilities, and will be the last procedure." Rounding only happens **once, at the very end**.

> [!tip] In this example, if that 10% rating were instead a 20% (maybe through an increase claim), the total would be 72 + 5.6 = 77.6%, which rounds to **80%** -- a full tier higher. Sometimes increasing an existing rating beats filing new claims.

sections.heading: :star: The Bilateral Factor (38 CFR 4.26)
sections.body:
Here's a hidden boost most veterans don't know about. If you have disabilities affecting **both arms, both legs, or paired extremities**, VA adds a **10% bilateral factor** before combining with other disabilities.

> [!important] The bilateral factor is automatic -- VA should apply it whenever you have qualifying paired disabilities. But mistakes happen. Know the math so you can verify.

---

**How it works:**
1. **Combine** the bilateral disabilities first
2. **Add** 10% of that combined value (not combine -- ADD)
3. Treat that result as a **single disability** and combine with remaining ratings

**Example: 20% right knee + 10% left knee + 50% PTSD**

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Combine bilateral knees | 20 + 10 = 28% (from table) |
| 2 | Add bilateral factor | 28 x 0.10 = 2.8, so 28 + 2.8 = **30.8** (round to 31 for combining) |
| 3 | Combine 50% with 31% | **66%** |
| 4 | Round to nearest 10 | **70% combined** |

> [!tip] Per `38 CFR 4.26(d)`, if including the bilateral factor actually produces a **lower result** than not including it, VA must use whichever calculation is **more favorable to you**. The bilateral factor can only help, never hurt.

> [!check] If you have any paired extremity conditions (both knees, both shoulders, both ankles), make sure VA is applying the bilateral factor. Check your rating codesheet -- it should show the bilateral factor calculation.

sections.heading: :brain: Rounding Rules
sections.body:
Rounding is where VA math gets really high-stakes. A single percentage point can mean the difference between rating tiers -- and hundreds of dollars per month.

> [!important] Rounding only happens **once** -- at the very end after ALL combining is done.

---

**The rules:**

| Combined Value Ends In | Rounds To | Example |
|-----------------------|-----------|--------|
| **5 through 9** | UP to next 10 | 65% becomes **70%** |
| **1 through 4** | DOWN to previous 10 | 64% becomes **60%** |
| **0.5** | UP per VA interpretation | 74.5% becomes **75%**, then **80%** |

> [!danger] The difference between a combined **74.9% and 75.0%** is the difference between a **70% and 80% combined rating**. That can be **$400+ per month** in compensation. Know exactly where your combined rating falls before rounding.

> [!check] Understanding rounding helps you decide which claims to prioritize. If you're sitting at 73% combined, you need claims that push you past 75%. If you're at 76%, you're already rounding to 80% -- focus on getting past 85% next.

sections.heading: :money: Key Rating Thresholds and What They Unlock
sections.body:
Certain combined rating levels are game-changers that trigger additional benefits beyond just higher monthly compensation. Know these thresholds.

---

| Rating | What It Unlocks |
|--------|----------------|
| **30%** | **Dependent allowances** (spouse, children, dependent parents). Below 30%, no dependent add-ons. |
| **50%** | Higher compensation with dependent allowances. Some **state benefits** trigger here. |
| **70%** | Meets one schedular threshold for **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)` (if one disability is 40%+) |
| **100%** | Full schedular rating. **Chapter 35 DEA**, **CHAMPVA**, **property tax exemptions**, and more. |
| **100% + SMC** | **Special Monthly Compensation** at housebound rate (SMC-S) if single disability at 100% plus additional at 60%+ |

> [!important] The jump from 20% to 30% is massive -- it unlocks **dependent allowances** that can add hundreds per month for a veteran with a spouse and children. If you're at 20%, getting to 30% should be priority #1.

> [!check] **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)` requires either one disability at **60%+** OR a combined rating of **70%+ with one disability at 40%+**. If you can't work due to service-connected conditions, TDIU pays at the **100% rate** even without a 100% schedular rating.

> [!tip] At 100%, your dependents may qualify for **Chapter 35 DEA** (up to 36 months of education benefits) and **CHAMPVA** healthcare. These benefits alone can be worth tens of thousands of dollars. Also check your state for property tax exemptions -- many states offer full exemptions at 100%.

sections.heading: :fire: Strategic Implications
sections.body:
Now that you understand the math, use it. Every filing decision should be informed by where you stand and what gets you to the next threshold.

> [!important] Because of VA math, each additional rating gives you **diminishing returns**. A veteran at 80% who adds a 10% disability only goes from 80% to 82% -- which still rounds to **80%**. That same veteran getting a 30% increased to 50% could push from 80% to 86% -- rounding to **90%**.

---

**Strategic priority order:**
1. Get your **highest-rated conditions rated as high as possible** first
2. Pursue **increase claims** on conditions rated below what the evidence supports
3. Don't ignore small ratings -- they can push you **past a rounding threshold**
4. Identify **secondary conditions** under `38 CFR 3.310` that stack bilateral factor benefits
5. If you're close to 100%, calculate whether **TDIU** under `38 CFR 4.16` gets you there faster

> [!check] Before filing anything new, run your current combined rating through a VA calculator and model what each potential claim would do to your combined. **File the claim that moves the needle most.**

> [!quote] A veteran at 82% combined (rounds to 80%) files for tinnitus at 10%. New combined: 83.8% -- still rounds to 80%. Zero net effect. That same veteran files an increase on their 30% knee to 40%. New combined: 86% -- rounds to 90%. That's the power of strategic filing.

> [!tip] Secondary conditions are often the fastest path to higher combined ratings. A service-connected back condition causing radiculopathy in both legs? That's two new ratings PLUS the bilateral factor. Model it out.

title: Appeals 101
desc: HLR, Supplemental, or Board -- which lane to pick, with form numbers, timelines, and strategy

sections.heading: :flag: The Three Appeal Lanes Under AMA
sections.body:
Got a denial? You have **three shots** under the Appeals Modernization Act (AMA). Each one is a different tool for a different situation -- pick wrong and you waste months.

Under `38 CFR 3.2500`, here are your options:

| Lane | Form | What It Does |
|------|------|--------------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | `VA Form 20-0995` | Submit **new and relevant evidence** for a fresh review |
| **Higher-Level Review** | `VA Form 20-0996` | A **senior reviewer** re-examines the same evidence for errors |
| **Board of Veterans' Appeals** | `VA Form 10182` | A **Veterans Law Judge** reviews your case |

> [!important] Each lane has different rules, different timelines, and different strategic advantages. Choosing the right one depends on **why you were denied** and **what evidence you have**.

> [!check] Before picking a lane, read your denial letter carefully. The **stated reasons for denial** tell you exactly which lane gives you the best shot.

sections.heading: :doc: Supplemental Claims: When You Have New Evidence
sections.body:
This is your go-to when you have **new evidence that wasn't previously considered**. File `VA Form 20-0995` and bring the goods.

**What counts as new and relevant evidence:**
- A new **medical opinion** or **nexus letter**
- Updated **treatment records** showing worsening
- A **buddy statement** corroborating your claim
- Newly obtained **service records**
- A new **diagnosis** addressing the denial reason

> [!important] The evidence must be "new and relevant" under `38 CFR 3.2501` -- it has to **directly address the reason your claim was denied**. If you were denied for no nexus, a new nexus letter is relevant. If denied for no current diagnosis, a new diagnosis is relevant.

**Key stats:**
- **No limit** on how many you can file
- **Average processing time:** 4-6 months
- Each filing just needs to identify or submit new and relevant evidence

> [!tip] VA's **full duty to assist** under `38 CFR 3.159` applies to Supplemental Claims. That means VA will help obtain records and may order new C&P exams. This is a major advantage over Higher-Level Review.

> [!check] If you have ANY new evidence that addresses the denial reason, this is almost always your best first move.

sections.heading: :search: Higher-Level Review: When VA Made an Error
sections.body:
VA messed up? A **senior reviewer examines the same record** with fresh eyes. No new evidence allowed -- file `VA Form 20-0996`.

**HLR is your move when:**
- VA **misapplied a regulation** or diagnostic code
- The rater **ignored favorable evidence** already in your file
- There was a **clear and unmistakable error** in the decision
- VA **failed its duty to assist** (didn't obtain records, didn't provide an adequate exam)
- The rating **doesn't match the evidence** already on record

> [!check] **Request the informal conference call** -- always take this option. It lets you speak directly with the senior reviewer and highlight the specific errors you've identified. This is your chance to say "look at page 47 of my C-file."

> [!warning] No new evidence means NO new evidence. Don't bring a new nexus letter to an HLR -- that gets rejected. If you have new evidence, file a Supplemental Claim instead.

**Processing time:** 2-4 months average

> [!tip] If the HLR reviewer finds a **duty-to-assist error**, the claim gets sent back to the regional office for correction. This is a common win scenario -- VA didn't do its job the first time, and the senior reviewer catches it.

sections.heading: :gavel: Board of Veterans' Appeals: The Big Guns
sections.body:
When the lower levels have failed you, or your case involves **complex legal issues**, bring it to a Veterans Law Judge. File `VA Form 10182`.

**The Board has three dockets:**

| Docket | New Evidence? | Hearing? | Avg. Timeline |
|--------|--------------|----------|----------------|
| **Direct Review** | No | No | 6-12 months |
| **Evidence Submission** | Yes (90 days) | No | 12-18 months |
| **Hearing** | Yes (90 days after) | Yes | 18-24+ months |

> [!tip] The **Hearing docket** has the highest win rate. Yes, the wait is longer, but you get to tell your story directly to the judge. If your case has any complexity or nuance, this is worth the wait.

> [!important] Board decisions carry **more legal weight** and can set precedent. A BVA grant is harder for VA to overturn than a regional office grant.

> [!check] If you've already been denied at HLR or Supplemental, the Board is your next stop. Don't give up -- many cases are won at the Board that were denied below.

sections.heading: :alert: Deadlines That Matter
sections.body:
This is where claims die. Miss a deadline, lose your money. It's that simple.

> [!danger] You have **ONE YEAR** from the date of your VA decision to file in any appeal lane while preserving your effective date. This is non-negotiable.

**The effective date math:**

| When You File | Effective Date | Back Pay? |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Within 1 year** | Original claim date (or ITF date) | **Full back pay** from that date |
| **After 1 year** | New filing date | **No back pay** from original period |

> [!warning] You can still file a Supplemental Claim after one year (there's no time limit on those), but your effective date **resets to the new filing date**. You lose all the back pay from the original period. That could be tens of thousands of dollars.

For Board appeals:
- File `VA Form 10182` **within one year**
- Evidence Submission/Hearing docket: **90 days** after filing to submit new evidence

> [!check] **Never let a deadline pass without action.** If you need more time to gather evidence, file your appeal NOW and submit evidence as it becomes available. An Intent to File or a filed form with evidence to follow beats a missed deadline every time.

sections.heading: :target: Which Lane to Choose: A Decision Framework
sections.body:
Stop guessing. Walk through this decision tree.

**Question 1: Do you have new evidence that addresses the denial reason?**
- **Yes** --> File a **Supplemental Claim** (`20-0995`)
- **No** --> Move to Question 2

**Question 2: Did VA make a clear error with the existing evidence?**
- **Yes** --> File a **Higher-Level Review** (`20-0996`)
- **No** --> Move to Question 3

**Question 3: Is your case legally complex, or denied at lower levels?**
- **Yes** --> File with the **Board** (`10182`) -- consider the Hearing docket

> [!tip] **You can lane-switch.** If your HLR is denied, file a Supplemental Claim with new evidence. If your Supplemental is denied, file with the Board. Each new decision starts a **fresh one-year clock** for the next filing.

> [!quote] Real scenario: Veteran denied for no nexus. Files HLR (wrong move -- no new evidence to fix the nexus gap). HLR denied. Then files Supplemental with a new nexus letter from a private doctor. Granted. The HLR cost 3 months, but the lane-switch saved the claim.

> [!check] When in doubt, ask yourself: "What was the specific reason for denial, and do I have something new to address it?" That answer tells you the lane.

sections.heading: :lock: Clear and Unmistakable Error (CUE)
sections.body:
CUE is the **nuclear option** -- it attacks a **final VA decision** and can unlock **years or decades of back pay**.

Under `38 CFR 3.105(a)`, if you prove a prior final decision contained clear and unmistakable error, VA must revise the decision and pay you back to the original effective date.

**CUE requires proving ALL THREE elements:**

1. The **correct facts** as known at the time were not before the adjudicator, OR the **law was incorrectly applied**
2. The error was **undebatable** -- reasonable minds could not differ
3. The error was **outcome-determinative** -- but for the error, the result would have been different

> [!danger] CUE is an extremely high bar. It is NOT a disagreement with how VA weighed the evidence. It is NOT "they should have rated me higher." It's a **provable, undebatable mistake** in fact or law.

> [!warning] If you file a CUE motion and lose, it can actually hurt future arguments. Don't file CUE on a hunch or frustration.

> [!check] If you believe you have a CUE claim, **consult an experienced VA attorney** before filing. The stakes are enormous (potentially decades of back pay) and the standard is demanding. This is not a DIY claim.

sections.heading: :shield: Beyond BVA: The Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims
sections.body:
If the Board denies you, there's one more level: the **U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC)**. This is a federal court that reviews BVA decisions for **legal errors**.

> [!danger] You have **120 days** from the date of the BVA decision to file a Notice of Appeal with the CAVC. This deadline is **jurisdictional** -- miss it and the court literally cannot hear your case. No exceptions.

**What to know about CAVC:**
- Typically **requires an attorney** (many work on contingency)
- The court can **affirm, reverse, or remand** the BVA decision
- Most successful appeals result in a **remand** -- sending the case back to BVA with instructions to fix the legal error

> [!important] Landmark CAVC cases that shaped veterans' rights:
- **Nieves-Rodriguez v. Peake** (No. 06-312) -- medical opinion adequacy standards
- **Jandreau v. Nicholson** (No. 04-1254) -- lay evidence competency
- **Euzebio v. McDonough** (No. 17-2879) -- TDIU evidence requirements

> [!tip] If you're considering a CAVC appeal, contact a veterans' law attorney immediately after the BVA denial. The 120-day clock starts ticking the day the decision is mailed, and you need time to find and retain counsel.

sections.heading: :chart: Timeline Expectations
sections.body:
Set realistic expectations so you're not calling the VA hotline every week.

| Appeal Lane | Average Processing Time | Notes |
|-------------|------------------------|-------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | 4-6 months | Faster with strong, straightforward evidence |
| **Higher-Level Review** | 2-4 months | Fastest -- no new evidence development needed |
| **Board (Direct Review)** | 6-12 months | Fastest Board docket |
| **Board (Evidence Submission)** | 12-18 months | Mid-range |
| **Board (Hearing)** | 18-24+ months | Longest wait, highest win rate |
| **CAVC Appeal** | 12-24 months | After filing Notice of Appeal |

> [!warning] These are **averages** -- individual cases vary significantly. Complex cases with multiple issues take longer. Simple single-issue claims can resolve faster.

> [!check] **Track your claim** on VA.gov and contact your representative if it stalls beyond the expected timeframe. Squeaky wheel gets the grease -- but give it reasonable time first.

> [!tip] While waiting, keep building your evidence. Get current treatment, document symptoms, and collect buddy statements. If you win a remand, you'll be ready to hit the ground running.

title: Increase Claims
desc: When and how to file for a higher rating -- with DeLuca factors, reduction protections, and strategic timing

sections.heading: :fire: When to File for an Increase
sections.body:
Your condition got worse. You deserve a higher rating. But **timing matters** -- file smart, not just fast.

**Signs it's time to file:**
- New symptoms have appeared
- Existing symptoms are **more frequent or severe**
- You're on **new or stronger medications**
- You need **additional or different treatment**
- Your condition impacts **daily activities and work** more than before
- Your **range of motion has decreased**
- You've had new **hospitalizations or emergency visits**

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 3.400(o)(2)`, if it's factually ascertainable that an increase occurred **within one year before you file**, VA can make the effective date retroactive to the date of the increase. This means you can get back pay from when the worsening actually happened.

> [!check] Don't sit on a worsening condition. File promptly when symptoms increase -- every month you wait is a month of higher compensation you might lose.

sections.heading: :book: Know Your Rating Criteria First
sections.body:
Before you file anything, **look up what the next higher rating actually requires**. This is the single most important prep step.

Find your diagnostic code in `38 CFR Part 4` and study the criteria. You need to know the exact threshold you're aiming for.

**Example -- Lumbar Spine (DC 5237/5242):**

| Rating | Forward Flexion Requirement |
|--------|---------------------------|
| 10% | Greater than 60 degrees |
| 20% | Greater than 30 but not greater than 60 degrees |
| 40% | 30 degrees or less |
| 50% | Unfavorable ankylosis |

> [!quote] If you're at 10% and your flexion has dropped to 55 degrees, you may qualify for 20%. Knowing this BEFORE you file tells you exactly what to document.

**Example -- PTSD (DC 9411):**

| Rating | Impairment Level |
|--------|------------------|
| 30% | Occasional decrease in work efficiency |
| 50% | Reduced reliability and productivity |
| 70% | Deficiencies in **most areas** (work, family, judgment, thinking, mood) |
| 100% | **Total** occupational and social impairment |

> [!tip] Print out the rating criteria for your diagnostic code and bring it to your doctor. Ask them to document findings that specifically address the criteria for the next higher level. Doctors don't know VA rating language unless you show them.

sections.heading: :tools: Building Your Evidence
sections.body:
Don't file naked. **Build your paper trail before submitting** the claim.

**Your evidence checklist:**

1. **Current treatment records** from VA or private providers noting increased severity
2. **Physician statement** describing how the condition has declined since the last rating
3. **Personal statement** documenting functional limitations -- what you can no longer do
4. **Buddy statements** from family or coworkers describing changes they've observed

> [!tip] The strongest increase claims pair **recent medical evidence showing worsening** with **lay statements describing real-world functional impact**. VA raters look at both the clinical findings and how the condition affects your ability to work and live.

> [!warning] Don't assume your VA treatment records tell the whole story. If you only go to the VA when things are really bad, your records may show gaps that make it look like you're doing fine. Get consistent treatment documented.

> [!check] Before filing, gather ALL four evidence types listed above. A claim with just a form and no supporting evidence is a claim that gets a quick denial.

sections.heading: :star: The DeLuca Factors: Pain, Flare-ups, and Functional Loss
sections.body:
For **musculoskeletal conditions**, the DeLuca factors are your secret weapon. Most veterans don't know about them -- and most C&P examiners don't fully capture them.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 4.40`, **functional loss from pain** is a compensable disability factor. Under `38 CFR 4.45`, VA must consider **weakened movement, excess fatigability, incoordination, and pain on movement**.

`38 CFR 4.59` adds that "actually painful, unstable, or malaligned joints, due to healed injury" are entitled to **at least the minimum compensable rating** for that joint. Pain on both **active and passive motion**, in **weight-bearing and nonweight-bearing**, must be tested.

> [!tip] **Flare-ups are where the money is.** If your condition is worse during flare-ups than what the examiner sees on exam day, the examiner **must estimate the additional functional loss** during flare-ups. If your back flexion is 50 degrees in the office but drops to 20 degrees during weekly flare-ups, that additional loss should be captured.

> [!quote] Example: Veteran's back measures 50 degrees flexion at the exam (10% criteria). But veteran reports weekly flare-ups where flexion drops to approximately 20 degrees. If the examiner properly documents flare-up impact, that's potentially 40% criteria -- a massive difference.

> [!check] At your C&P exam, specifically describe your **worst days** and **flare-up frequency**. Don't let the examiner skip the DeLuca factors.

sections.heading: :search: The C&P Exam for Increases
sections.body:
VA will schedule a new C&P exam to evaluate current severity. This exam can make or break your increase claim.

**What the examiner will do:**
- Compare your current state against the **rating criteria** in your diagnostic code
- Fill out the same **DBQ** used for initial claims
- Document **range of motion**, functional limitations, flare-up frequency/impact
- Record condition-specific factors

> [!warning] The same rules apply as initial claims: **describe your worst days, bring documentation, and don't minimize**. Veterans have a bad habit of toughing it out at the exam. This is not the time for that.

> [!check] If the exam happens on a good day, **tell the examiner explicitly**: "Today is actually one of my better days. On my worst days, which happen X times per week, my symptoms are..." The examiner should document your **typical and worst-day function**, not just what they see in 20 minutes.

> [!tip] Bring a **written summary** of your symptoms, limitations, and flare-up patterns. Hand it to the examiner. C&P exams are rushed -- a written summary ensures nothing gets missed.

sections.heading: :shield: Rating Protections: The 5, 10, and 20-Year Rules
sections.body:
VA can't just yank your rating whenever they want. **Federal regulations protect your existing ratings** under specific conditions.

| Rule | CFR Citation | Protection |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **5-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.344(a)-(b)` | Ratings in effect 5+ years can't be reduced unless evidence shows **sustained improvement** -- not just one good exam |
| **10-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.957` | Service connection in effect 10+ years **cannot be severed** except on showing of fraud or lack of qualifying service |
| **20-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.951(b)` | A rating continuously in effect 20+ years **cannot be reduced** below that level except on showing of fraud |

> [!important] The **5-Year Rule** is the most commonly relevant. VA must review the **entire record** and determine that improvement is reasonably certain to continue under **ordinary life conditions**. A single improved exam is NOT enough.

> [!tip] Know how long your rating has been in effect. If it's been 5+ years, you have **significantly stronger protection** against reduction. This changes your risk calculation when filing for an increase.

sections.heading: :alert: Watch Out for Proposed Reductions
sections.body:
Here's the catch: **filing for an increase opens your entire rating for that condition to review**. If the C&P exam shows improvement, VA can propose a reduction.

> [!danger] This is a real risk. Before filing for an increase, honestly assess whether your condition has improved in ANY area. If it has, weigh the risk of a reduction against the potential gain.

**If VA proposes a reduction, you have rights:**

1. VA must **notify you in writing** with a proposed rating and the evidence supporting it
2. You have **60 days** to submit evidence and argument against the reduction
3. You can **request a hearing** within 30 days
4. The reduction **cannot take effect** for 60 days after the final decision

> [!important] For ratings in effect 5+ years, `38 CFR 3.344(a)` requires VA to show **sustained improvement demonstrated over time** -- not just one favorable exam. If the reduction exam was **less thorough** than the original exam, that alone may be grounds to fight the reduction.

> [!check] If you receive a proposed reduction, **do not ignore it**. You have 60 days to fight -- use every one of them. Get medical evidence, lay statements, and cite the regulatory protections.

sections.heading: :money: Temporary 100% Ratings
sections.body:
Two regulations provide **temporary 100% ratings** that most veterans don't know about. Free money left on the table.

| Regulation | Trigger | What You Get |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `38 CFR 4.29` | Hospitalized **21+ consecutive days** for a service-connected condition | **Temporary 100%** for the duration of hospitalization |
| `38 CFR 4.30` | Surgery/treatment requiring **convalescence** for a service-connected condition | **Temporary 100%** during recovery |

**`38 CFR 4.30` convalescence applies when:**
- Treatment results in **surgical wounds that haven't healed**
- You need **home confinement**
- You **can't use a major joint or extremity**

> [!tip] These temporary ratings are **in addition to** your regular combined rating. They're based on specific dates of hospitalization or surgery, so back pay depends on when you file.

> [!check] Had surgery or a long hospitalization for a service-connected condition? File for a temporary 100% rating NOW. Don't leave this money on the table.

sections.heading: :target: What to Do If VA Proposes a Reduction
sections.body:
You got the dreaded proposed reduction letter. Don't panic -- **act fast and fight smart**.

**Your 60-day battle plan:**

1. **Request your complete C-file** (claims file) to see what evidence VA is relying on
2. **Compare the reduction exam** to the original exam -- is the new one as thorough?
3. **Get a current medical opinion** from your treating physician describing your actual functional limitations
4. **Submit a detailed written response** within 60 days citing specific regulatory protections
5. **Request a predetermination hearing** if appropriate

**In your response, argue these points:**
- Improvement has **not been sustained** (cite `38 CFR 3.344` if rated 5+ years)
- The reduction exam was **less complete** than the exam that established the rating
- The condition is subject to **temporary improvement** and the exam caught a good day
- **Lay evidence** from family, coworkers, or employer shows ongoing severity

> [!warning] If the reduction goes through despite your response, **appeal immediately** -- file a Supplemental Claim or Higher-Level Review within one year. Do not let the one-year deadline pass.

> [!check] The moment you receive a proposed reduction letter, start gathering evidence. You have 60 days -- use a calendar, set reminders, and treat this like a deadline that could cost you thousands.

title: TDIU Explained
desc: Getting paid at the 100% rate without a 100% schedular rating -- eligibility, evidence, and strategy

sections.heading: :star: What Is TDIU?
sections.body:
TDIU pays you at the **100% rate** even if your combined rating is less than 100%. It's the benefit most veterans don't know they qualify for.

Under `38 CFR 4.16`, TDIU exists because two veterans with the same 70% combined rating can have **vastly different functional capacities** -- one might work full-time while another can't hold any job.

> [!important] TDIU is for veterans whose service-connected disabilities prevent them from securing or following **"substantially gainful employment."** It's not about whether you can do any job in theory -- it's about whether your specific disabilities, education, work history, and training allow you to **actually earn a living**.

> [!check] If you're rated less than 100% but can't work because of your service-connected conditions, TDIU is your path to 100% compensation. Don't leave this money on the table.

sections.heading: :chart: Schedular Eligibility (38 CFR 4.16(a))
sections.body:
There are **two schedular thresholds** you need to meet. Hit either one and you're eligible.

| Threshold | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Single disability** | One service-connected disability rated at **60% or higher** |
| **Combined** | Combined rating of **70% or higher** with at least one disability at **40% or more** |

> [!important] For the "one disability at 60%" or "one at 40%" threshold, certain disabilities are **treated as a single disability**:
1. Disabilities of one or both **upper extremities**, or one or both **lower extremities** (including bilateral factor)
2. Disabilities from a **common etiology or single accident**
3. Disabilities affecting a **single body system** (e.g., all orthopedic, all neuropsychiatric)
4. Multiple injuries **incurred in action**
5. Multiple disabilities incurred as a **prisoner of war**

> [!quote] Example: You have a 30% right knee and 20% left knee from the same injury. They combine to a single **44% disability** (with bilateral factor) for TDIU threshold purposes. That meets the 40% single-disability requirement.

> [!tip] Run the grouping math before assuming you don't qualify. Many veterans who think they're short on thresholds actually meet them when disabilities are properly grouped.

sections.heading: :shield: Extraschedular TDIU (38 CFR 4.16(b))
sections.body:
Don't meet the schedular thresholds? **You can still get TDIU** through the extraschedular path.

Under `38 CFR 4.16(b)`, the regulation is clear: "all veterans who are unable to secure and follow a substantially gainful occupation by reason of service-connected disabilities **shall be rated totally disabled**." The rating board refers these cases to the **Director of Compensation Service** for extraschedular consideration.

> [!warning] Extraschedular TDIU is **harder to get** but not impossible. The evidence needs to be strong -- you can't just file the form and hope.

**What you need for extraschedular TDIU:**
- A **compelling vocational expert opinion** explaining why you can't work
- Clear **employment history** showing inability to maintain work
- **Medical evidence** documenting functional limitations that prevent employment
- Evidence that your situation is **exceptional** -- beyond what the rating schedule contemplates

> [!check] If your disabilities prevent you from working but you don't meet the schedular numbers, file anyway. VA is **required** to refer your case for extraschedular consideration if the evidence supports unemployability.

sections.heading: :brain: What 'Substantially Gainful Employment' Means
sections.body:
This is where most veterans get confused -- and where VA often gets it wrong.

> [!important] TDIU doesn't mean you **can't work at all**. It means your service-connected disabilities prevent you from earning above the **federal poverty threshold** through consistent employment.

Under `38 CFR 4.16(a)`, **"marginal employment shall not be considered substantially gainful employment."** Marginal employment exists when:
- Your earned annual income **doesn't exceed the poverty threshold** for one person
- You're employed in a **protected environment** (facts-found basis)

**Protected environment examples:**
- A **family business** with special accommodations
- A **sheltered workshop**
- A job where your employer makes **special accommodations** beyond what's normal

> [!tip] Working part-time, earning below the poverty threshold, or working in a protected environment **does NOT disqualify you** from TDIU. VA must consider the **nature of the employment** and the **reason for any termination**.

> [!warning] VA sometimes denies TDIU simply because a veteran has a job. That's wrong. The question is whether the employment is **substantially gainful** -- not whether it exists at all.

sections.heading: :tools: Key Evidence for Your TDIU Claim
sections.body:
File `VA Form 21-8940` (Application for Increased Compensation Based on Unemployability) and **build your evidence around these five pillars**:

**1. Employment History**
Document job losses, reduced hours, missed days, performance issues, and terminations due to your disabilities. Get **statements from former employers** about accommodations they provided or reasons they let you go.

**2. Functional Limitations**
Medical records documenting what you **physically and mentally cannot do**. Be specific: "cannot sit for more than 30 minutes," "cannot concentrate for sustained periods," "misses 4+ days per month due to flare-ups."

**3. Vocational Expert Opinion**
A vocational rehabilitation specialist can assess your education, training, work history, and functional limitations to opine on your employability. **This is often the strongest single piece of evidence.**

**4. Lay Statements**
Buddy statements from family, former coworkers, and friends who can describe how your disabilities affect your daily functional capacity and ability to maintain employment.

**5. VA and Private Treatment Records**
Ongoing treatment records showing the **severity and persistence** of your conditions.

> [!tip] The vocational expert opinion is the game-changer. If you can afford one, get one. It translates your medical conditions into **employability language** that VA raters understand.

> [!check] Don't file `21-8940` alone with no supporting evidence. Build your package with ALL five evidence types before submitting.

sections.heading: :target: TDIU with a Single Disability vs. Combined
sections.body:
Your strategy changes depending on whether you're claiming TDIU based on **one disability or a combination**.

**Single disability at 60%+:**
- Most straightforward path
- Show that **this one condition alone** prevents you from working
- Focus ALL employment and functional evidence on the impact of this specific disability

**Combined 70% with one at 40%+:**
- Argue the **combined effect** of your service-connected disabilities prevents employment
- Present evidence showing how **multiple conditions interact**

> [!quote] Example: PTSD causes sleep deprivation, which worsens chronic pain, which limits physical capacity, which triggers anxiety. The synergistic effect of all conditions together makes employment impossible, even though no single condition alone would.

> [!important] Remember the **grouping rules** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)`: disabilities from common etiology, a single body system, or affecting paired extremities count as "one disability" for threshold purposes. This can help you meet the 40% or 60% single-disability requirement even when individual ratings are lower.

> [!tip] When arguing combined effect, paint the picture of a **full day in your life**. Show how conditions cascade and compound each other from morning to night.

sections.heading: :link: TDIU and Social Security Disability
sections.body:
VA disability and Social Security Disability (SSDI/SSI) are **separate programs with different standards** -- but they can powerfully reinforce each other.

| Factor | VA TDIU | Social Security |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Standard | Can't secure/follow substantially gainful employment | Can't engage in substantial gainful activity |
| Based on | Service-connected disabilities only | All disabilities |
| Binding on each other? | No | No |
| Can receive both? | **Yes** | **Yes** |

> [!important] An SSA finding of disability is **not binding on VA**, and VA cannot deny TDIU solely because you don't receive Social Security disability. However, an SSA disability determination is **relevant evidence that VA must consider**.

> [!tip] If you're receiving SSDI, submit your **SSA disability determination letter**, **functional capacity evaluation**, and any **vocational assessments** from your SSA case as evidence in your VA TDIU claim. Another federal agency agreeing you can't work is powerful.

> [!check] You can receive **both** VA disability compensation (including TDIU) and Social Security disability simultaneously. They are **not offset** against each other. Apply for both.

sections.heading: :gavel: Euzebio v. McDonough: A Game-Changing CAVC Decision
sections.body:
This case changed the TDIU landscape. If you've been denied TDIU, you need to know about it.

The CAVC's **Euzebio decision** (Case No. 17-2879) strengthened veterans' rights on TDIU claims by requiring VA to consider **ALL evidence of unemployability** -- not just a single C&P exam.

> [!warning] Before Euzebio, VA would sometimes deny TDIU based primarily on **one C&P examiner's opinion** that the veteran could work, while ignoring lay statements, employment history, vocational opinions, and treatment records that supported unemployability.

> [!important] After Euzebio, VA must weigh the **totality of the evidence**. A single C&P exam opinion cannot override a mountain of other evidence showing unemployability.

> [!check] If you've been denied TDIU based primarily on one C&P exam while other evidence supports unemployability, **cite Euzebio specifically** in your appeal brief or hearing testimony. This case gives you strong grounds for appeal.

sections.heading: :eye: Protected Work and Marginal Employment
sections.body:
Currently working? **That doesn't automatically kill your TDIU claim.** VA must look beyond the mere fact of employment.

**Protected employment situations that may still qualify for TDIU:**
- Working for a **family business** with special accommodations (flexible hours, no productivity requirements, modified duties)
- **Sheltered workshop** environments
- **Part-time work** earning below the poverty threshold
- Employment with **extraordinary accommodations** from the employer
- **Self-employment** where you control your schedule around your disabilities

> [!tip] **Document every accommodation you receive.** If your employer lets you come in late after bad nights, take unscheduled breaks, miss days without consequence, or work reduced hours -- get a **written statement from them** describing these accommodations. This evidence shows your employment is "marginal" even if you technically have a job.

> [!quote] Example: Veteran works 15 hours/week at his brother's shop. Brother lets him leave whenever pain flares up, doesn't track hours, and pays him regardless of output. That's protected/marginal employment -- not substantially gainful.

> [!check] If you're working in a protected environment, get your employer to document the accommodations in writing using `VA Form 21-4192`. This form asks employers about accommodations, missed time, and special considerations.

sections.heading: :medal: Filing Strategy and Next Steps
sections.body:
Ready to file? Here's your complete TDIU battle plan.

**The TDIU filing checklist:**

1. **Check eligibility**: Do you meet `38 CFR 4.16(a)` schedular thresholds? If not, you'll need the extraschedular path
2. **File `VA Form 21-8940`** completely -- employment history, education, training, and detailed description of how **each disability** prevents work
3. **File `VA Form 21-4192`** for each employer in the last 5 years (or have your former employers complete it)
4. **Submit medical evidence** documenting functional limitations that prevent employment
5. **Get a vocational expert opinion** if possible -- this is often the strongest single piece of evidence
6. **Submit lay statements** from people who observe your daily limitations

> [!warning] Don't wait for the "perfect" evidence package. If you're eligible and can't work, **file an Intent to File** to lock in your effective date while you gather supporting documentation.

> [!check] If you're rated at 60%+ (single) or 70%+ (combined with one at 40%), and you can't work because of your service-connected conditions, **file for TDIU now**. Every month you delay is a month of 100% compensation you're not receiving.

title: Nexus Letters
desc: What makes a nexus letter actually work -- the standard, the case law, and how to get one that wins

sections.heading: :doc: What a Nexus Letter Must Contain
sections.body:
A nexus letter is the **single most important piece of evidence** in most disability claims. It is a medical opinion that connects your current condition to your military service. Without one, you are asking VA to connect the dots on their own -- and they will not.

> [!important] Every effective nexus letter needs exactly three elements -- miss one and the whole thing falls apart.

**The Three Required Elements:**

1. **A clear current diagnosis** of the claimed condition
2. **Identification of the in-service event**, injury, or exposure that caused or contributed to it
3. **A nexus opinion** using the required standard: **"at least as likely as not"** (50% or greater probability) that the current condition is related to the identified in-service event

> [!warning] A diagnosis without a nexus opinion is just a medical record. An opinion without a rationale is just a bare conclusion. Both get tossed.

VA raters and BVA judges evaluate nexus letters against **specific legal standards**. They are not just reading for vibes -- they are checking boxes. A weak letter does not just fail to help; it can actively hurt your claim by giving the rater ammunition to deny.

> [!check] Before you submit any nexus letter, verify it contains all three elements. If it is missing even one, send it back for revision.

sections.heading: :gavel: The Standard of Proof: 'At Least as Likely as Not'
sections.body:
VA uses the **"at least as likely as not"** standard -- a **50/50 probability or better**. This is a much lower bar than most people realize. The doctor does not need to say it is certain, probable, or even more likely than not. They just need to hit that 50% threshold.

> [!tip] Think of it like a coin flip. If your doctor can say there is at least a 50% chance your condition is connected to service, that meets the standard. You do not need a slam dunk.

| Language That **PASSES** | Language That **FAILS** |
|---|---|
| "It is at least as likely as not that..." | "It is possible that..." (too speculative) |
| "There is a 50% or greater probability that..." | "The condition could be related to..." (does not reach 50%) |
| "More likely than not, the veteran's condition is related to..." | "It is not unlikely that..." (ambiguous) |
| | "The condition may be related to..." (does not commit) |

> [!danger] The word **"possible"** is a claim killer. If your doctor writes "it is possible" instead of "it is at least as likely as not," your nexus letter is legally worthless. Same goes for "could be," "might be," and "may be."

> [!check] Coach your doctor on the exact language needed. Print out the passing phrases above and hand them to your provider. Many physicians are unfamiliar with VA's evidentiary standard and default to cautious medical language that does not meet the threshold.

sections.heading: :star: The Nieves-Rodriguez Standard: What Makes an Opinion Adequate
sections.body:
This is the case law that controls everything. **Nieves-Rodriguez v. Peake (CAVC No. 06-312)** established the gold standard for evaluating whether a medical opinion actually means anything.

> [!important] The court held that a medical opinion's probative value comes from its **reasoning** -- not the examiner's credentials or bare conclusions. A one-sentence opinion from a Harvard specialist loses to a detailed opinion from a family doctor.

**The Three Nieves-Rodriguez Factors:**

| Factor | What It Means | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Factually informed** | Did the examiner review the relevant records? | An opinion based on wrong facts is worthless |
| **Fully articulated** | Is the reasoning actually explained? | "I think so" is not an explanation |
| **Reasoned analysis** | Does the rationale connect evidence to conclusion? | The logic chain must be visible |

> [!warning] A one-paragraph nexus letter that says "in my opinion, the condition is related to service" without explaining **WHY** carries almost no weight -- even from a board-certified specialist. VA will assign it little to no probative value.

> [!quote] BVA judges routinely cite Nieves-Rodriguez when granting claims based on well-reasoned private opinions over poorly-reasoned VA exam opinions. A detailed letter with a thorough rationale from a general practitioner can be **highly persuasive**.

> [!check] When reviewing your nexus letter draft, check each Nieves-Rodriguez factor. Does the letter show the doctor reviewed YOUR records? Does it explain the medical reasoning? Does the conclusion follow logically from the evidence discussed?

sections.heading: :shield: What Makes a Nexus Letter Strong
sections.body:
Not all nexus letters are created equal. Here is what separates a letter that wins from one that gets dismissed in a single sentence by the rater.

**The Five Pillars of a Strong Nexus Letter:**

- **Detailed rationale** explaining the medical reasoning for the connection. Not just a conclusion, but the chain of logic: the veteran had X exposure in service, medical literature shows X causes Y, the veteran's timeline is consistent with this causation

- **Peer-reviewed medical literature** supporting the connection. Cite specific studies, journal articles, or established medical principles -- not just "it is well known that..."

- **Discussion of YOUR specific history** and symptom timeline. The opinion should show the doctor actually knows your case, not that they used a template

- **Acknowledgment and rebuttal of negative evidence**. If there is a gap in treatment or a prior negative opinion, address it head-on

- **The doctor's qualifications** and relevant expertise. A board-certified orthopedic surgeon's opinion on a joint condition carries more weight than a family medicine physician's

> [!tip] The strongest nexus letters read like a mini research paper about YOUR case. They cite your specific records, reference medical literature, explain the mechanism of injury, and address any counterarguments. If your letter could apply to any veteran by swapping the name, it is too generic.

> [!check] Ask your doctor to include at least **two peer-reviewed citations** and to specifically discuss your **service treatment records** and **post-service medical timeline** by date. Generic letters get generic denials.

sections.heading: :alert: Common Reasons Nexus Letters Fail
sections.body:
VA and BVA reject nexus letters for predictable, avoidable reasons. Know these failure modes so you can catch them before you submit.

| Failure Mode | What Happens | How to Fix It |
|---|---|---|
| **Speculative language** | "Might be," "could be," or "possibly related" | Use "at least as likely as not" |
| **No rationale** | Bare conclusion without explanation | Add detailed medical reasoning |
| **Template/boilerplate** | Fill-in-the-blank form letter | Personalize with specific case details |
| **Inaccurate facts** | Wrong service history or record errors | Have veteran review draft for accuracy |
| **Ignores gaps** | 20-year gap between service and diagnosis | Explain the gap with evidence |
| **Unqualified provider** | Wrong specialty for the condition | Match provider specialty to condition |
